summary:
The agent identifies rotational recombination as central to the phenomenon where two co-infected viruses contribute their genomic segments simultaneously within one host cell. This leads to the formation of a genetically diverse, stable offspring virus—a hallmark feature observed specifically in segmented RNA viruses such as rotavirus.

The agent concludes that rotavirus best explains the proposed mechanism involving simultaneous generation and random assembly of disparate genome segments from two infecting viruses, which results in emergent virulence traits typical of reassorted pathogens.  

Only rotaviruses have segmented genomes allowing for efficient reassortment upon dual infection—the core reason behind emerging pathogenicity—and thus aligns precisely with the described biological pathway.
